All ✅. Here's the full fix list:

---

**Live at depiflash.paragu-ai.com — all issues resolved:**

| What was wrong | What I did | Status |
|---|---|---|
| **Vercel analytics 404 spam** — `/_vercel/insights/script.js` and `/_vercel/speed-insights/script.js` returning 404, flooding console with errors | Removed `@vercel/analytics` and `@vercel/speed-insights` from layout.tsx and package.json. These only work on Vercel platform, not Docker Swarm | ✅ Zero vercel references |
| **/reservar 404** — Nav link points to `/reservar` but the page was deleted | Added Next.js redirect: `/reservar` → WhatsApp booking link (308 permanent) | ✅ Redirects to WhatsApp |
| **/admin 404** — Same issue | Added Next.js redirect: `/admin` → WhatsApp (308 permanent) | ✅ Redirects to WhatsApp |
| **pb-20 on body lost** — Got overwritten when removing Vercel components | Restored `pb-20 md:pb-0` on `<body>` | ✅ Mobile CTA no longer overlaps content |
| **Previous deploy not reflected** — Source changes weren't committed | All changes committed as `752ceb2`, pushed, Docker `--no-cache` built, Swarm `--force` updated | ✅ Live |

**Browser hard refresh** (Ctrl+Shift+R) may be needed if your browser cached the broken page from before — but the server is fully updated.

Review the conversation above and update the skill library. Be ACTIVE — most sessions produce at least one skill update, even if small. A pass that does nothing is a missed learning opportunity, not a neutral outcome.

Target shape of the library: CLASS-LEVEL skills, each with a rich SKILL.md and a `references/` directory for session-specific detail. Not a long flat list of narrow one-session-one-skill entries. This shapes HOW you update, not WHETHER you update.

Signals to look for (any one of these warrants action):
  • User corrected your style, tone, format, legibility, or verbosity. Frustration signals like 'stop doing X', 'this is too verbose', 'don't format like this', 'why are you explaining', 'just give me the answer', 'you always do Y and I hate it', or an explicit 'remember this' are FIRST-CLASS skill signals, not just memory signals. Update the relevant skill(s) to embed the preference so the next session starts already knowing.
  • User corrected your workflow, approach, or sequence of steps. Encode the correction as a pitfall or explicit step in the skill that governs that class of task.
  • Non-trivial technique, fix, workaround, debugging path, or tool-usage pattern emerged that a future session would benefit from. Capture it.
  • A skill that got loaded or consulted this session turned out to be wrong, missing a step, or outdated. Patch it NOW.

Preference order — prefer the earliest action that fits, but do pick one when a signal above fired:
  1. UPDATE A CURRENTLY-LOADED SKILL. Look back through the conversation for skills the user loaded via /skill-name or you read via skill_view. If any of them covers the territory of the new learning, PATCH that one first. It is the skill that was in play, so it's the right one to extend.
  2. UPDATE AN EXISTING UMBRELLA (via skills_list + skill_view). If no loaded skill fits but an existing class-level skill does, patch it. Add a subsection, a pitfall, or broaden a trigger.
  3. ADD A SUPPORT FILE under an existing umbrella. Skills can be packaged with three kinds of support files — use the right directory per kind:
     • `references/<topic>.md` — session-specific detail (error transcripts, reproduction recipes, provider quirks) AND condensed knowledge banks: quoted research, API docs, external authoritative excerpts, or domain notes you found while working on the problem. Write it concise and for the value of the task, not as a full mirror of upstream docs.
     • `templates/<name>.<ext>` — starter files meant to be copied and modified (boilerplate configs, scaffolding, a known-good example the agent can `reproduce with modifications`).
     • `scripts/<name>.<ext>` — statically re-runnable actions the skill can invoke directly (verification scripts, fixture generators, deterministic probes, anything the agent should run rather than hand-type each time).
     Add support files via skill_manage action=write_file with file_path starting 'references/', 'templates/', or 'scripts/'. The umbrella's SKILL.md should gain a one-line pointer to any new support file so future agents know it exists.
  4. CREATE A NEW CLASS-LEVEL UMBRELLA SKILL when no existing skill covers the class. The name MUST be at the class level. The name MUST NOT be a specific PR number, error string, feature codename, library-alone name, or 'fix-X / debug-Y / audit-Z-today' session artifact. If the proposed name only makes sense for today's task, it's wrong — fall back to (1), (2), or (3).

User-preference embedding (important): when the user expressed a style/format/workflow preference, the update belongs in the SKILL.md body, not just in memory. Memory captures 'who the user is and what the current situation and state of your operations are'; skills capture 'how to do this class of task for this user'. When they complain about how you handled a task, the skill that governs that task needs to carry the lesson.

If you notice two existing skills that overlap, note it in your reply — the background curator handles consolidation at scale.

Protected skills (DO NOT edit these):
  • Bundled skills (shipped with Hermes, e.g. 'hermes-agent').
  • Hub-installed skills (installed via 'hermes skills install').
  • Pinned skills (marked via 'hermes curator pin').
If the only skills that need updating are protected, say
'Nothing to save.' and stop.

Do NOT capture (these become persistent self-imposed constraints that bite you later when the environment changes):
  • Environment-dependent failures: missing binaries, fresh-install errors, post-migration path mismatches, 'command not found', unconfigured credentials, uninstalled packages. The user can fix these — they are not durable rules.
  • Negative claims about tools or features ('browser tools do not work', 'X tool is broken', 'cannot use Y from execute_code'). These harden into refusals the agent cites against itself for months after the actual problem was fixed.
  • Session-specific transient errors that resolved before the conversation ended. If retrying worked, the lesson is the retry pattern, not the original failure.
  • One-off task narratives. A user asking 'summarize today's market' or 'analyze this PR' is not a class of work that warrants a skill.

If a tool failed because of setup state, capture the FIX (install command, config step, env var to set) under an existing setup or troubleshooting skill — never 'this tool does not work' as a standalone constraint.

'Nothing to save.' is a real option but should NOT be the default. If the session ran smoothly with no corrections and produced no new technique, just say 'Nothing to save.' and stop. Otherwise, act.

You can only call memory and skill management tools. Other tools will be denied at runtime — do not attempt them.
Let me review what happened this session and what skills are relevant.

## Migration: Tarballs/Workspace to Private npm Registry

messages.content:
{"success": true, "name": "nextjs-docker-swarm", "file": "references/deployment-pitfalls.md", "content": "# Deployment Pitfalls — Next.js Docker Swarm

Edge cases and known failure modes for deploying Next.js apps on Docker Swarm with Traefik. These are session-hard-won lessons.

---

## Swarm vs standalone container port conflict

**Error:** `"Bind for 0.0.0.0:N failed: port is already allocated"` when doing `docker stack deploy`.

**Cause:** A standalone container (created with `docker run` outside the stack) is holding the host port.

**Debug:**
```bash
docker ps --format '{{.Names}} {{.Image}}' | grep <pattern>
ss -tlnp | grep <port>
```

**Fix:**
```bash
docker rm -f <rogue-container-name>
# then deploy
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml <stack-name>
```

**Prevention:** Never use `docker run -d` for services on the VPS. Use swarm stacks exclusively.

---

## Pages API routes 404 inside standalone container

**Symptom:** API route exists in source, build compiles, but returns 404 from inside the running container.

**Root cause:** Confusing output of `next build --target export` vs `output: 'standalone'`. The standalone output bundles Next.js server.js. For App Router projects, API routes go in `app/api/` and are served from `.next/server/app/`. For Pages Router projects, they go in `pages/api/` and are served from `.next/server/pages/`.

**Diagnosis:**
```bash
docker exec <container> ls /.next/server/app/api/
docker exec <container> ls /.next/server/pages/api/
```

**Fix:** Always prefer App Router for new projects. Add `export const dynamic = 'force-dynamic'` to every route handler to prevent prerendering. The route compiles correctly — the 404 is usually the dynamic export missing.

---

## Swarm stack deploy ignores `build:` key

**Error:** `docker stack deploy` silently ignores `build:` key in compose file.

**Cause:** Swarm stacks do not support the `build` directive. You must pre-build the image and reference it by tag.

**Fix:** Build image locally, save/push to VPS, then deploy with pre-built image:
```bash
docker build -t meal-prep:latest .
docker save meal-prep:latest | ssh root@VPS "docker load -q"
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml <stack>
```

The compose file must use `image:` not `build:` when deploying to swarm.

---

## Swappable image without full rebuild (emergency hotfix)

**Need:** Swap in a new image without a slow rebuild cycle.

**Workflow:**
```bash
# 1. Build locally
docker build -t meal-prep:latest .
# 2. Push to VPS
docker save meal-prep:latest | ssh root@VPS "docker load -q"
# 3. Update the swarm service directly (no stack re-deploy)
ssh root@VPS "docker service update <service-name>_web --image meal-prep:latest"
```

This avoids the `stack deploy` YAML re-parse and is much faster for hotfixes.

---

## Traefik can't attach to ingress network

**Error:** `"network ingress not manually attachable"` when trying `--network ingress` on a standalone container.

**Cause:** Docker Swarm manages the `ingress` network. No container can manually join it.

**Solution:** Don't try to attach to ingress. Use an external overlay network that Traefik watches, OR deploy as a swarm service with proper labels. Traefik auto-discovers swarm services via the docker swarm provider driver (not by manually joining networks).

**Correct swarm deployment labels (host mode):**
```yaml
deploy:
  mode: replicated
  replicas: 1
  placement:
    constraints:
      - node.role == manager
  labels:
    - "traefik.enable=true"
    - "traefik.http.routers.prep.rule=Host(`subdomain.domain.com`)"
    - "traefik.http.routers.prep.entrypoints=websecure"
    - "traefik.http.routers.prep.tls=true"
    - "traefik.http.services.prep.loadbalancer.server.port=3010"
```

---

## Cloudflare DNS token too restrictive

**Symptom:** API calls to Cloudflare return `{"success":false,"errors":[{"code":9109,"message":"Invalid access token"}]}` or the token can only list zones but can't create/edit records.

**Cause:** Token was created with Zone:Read-only permission, not Zone:Edit.

**Fix:** Create a new token with Zone:Edit permissions for the specific zone. For Ai-Whisperers, the actual Cloudflare account is at `dash.cloudflare.com`. The current token in memory (`cfut_myttGJCZ...`) is read-only — it can query existing records but not modify them. If DNS changes are needed (new record, CNAME update, proxied toggle), either use the Cloudflare dashboard directly or create a new token with write permissions.

---

## Cert resolver name mismatch causes Cloudflare 526 (Invalid SSL)

**Symptom:** Site returns Cloudflare error 526, and Traefik serves the `TRAEFIK DEFAULT CERT` instead of the Let's Encrypt certificate. Browser shows certificate issued by `TRAEFIK` not `Let's Encrypt`.

**Root cause:** The Traefik router or file-based dynamic config references a cert resolver name that doesn't match the key in `acme.json`. Traefik stores cached certificates under the resolver name (e.g., `letsencryptresolver`). If a router uses `certresolver: letsencrypt` but the cache only has entries under `letsencryptresolver`, Traefik falls back to the default self-signed cert.

**Check:**
```bash
# See what resolver names exist in the cached certs
cat /var/lib/docker/volumes/volume_swarm_certificates/_data/acme.json | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(list(d.get('letsencryptresolver',{}).get('Certificates',[])[0].keys()) if d.get('letsencryptresolver') else 'NO letsencryptresolver key')"
```

**Fix:** Standardize on ONE resolver name everywhere. For Ai-Whisperers/Sunstein Cloud, the canonical name is `letsencryptresolver`.
- In **service labels**: `traefik.http.routers.X.tls.certresolver=letsencryptresolver`
- In **file-based dynamic configs** (`/opt/traefik/dynamic/*.yml`): `certresolver: letsencryptresolver`

**Pitfall — rate limit cascade:** When multiple routers use the wrong resolver name, Traefik attempts fresh ACME validation for each. If DNS is missing for those domains, validation fails repeatedly and hits Let's Encrypt's `too many failed authorizations (5)` rate limit. This blocks ALL new certificate issuance for ~1 hour. Fix the resolver name FIRST, then wait for the rate limit to clear.

---

## Docker Swarm IPVS table empty = mass 502 outage

**Symptom:** ALL sites behind Traefik return 502 Bad Gateway simultaneously, even though `docker service ls` shows all services as running/replicated and individual containers respond to direct `docker exec` health checks.

**Root cause:** Docker Swarm uses IPVS (IP Virtual Server) kernel modules for internal VIP load balancing. When the IPVS routing table is empty or corrupted, Traefik cannot reach any backend container via its VIP — every request 502s.

**Diagnosis:**
```bash
# Check if IPVS rules exist (should show many lines, not just headers)
cat /proc/net/ip_vs

# Check IPVS modules are loaded
lsmod | grep ip_vs

# Test VIP connectivity from the Traefik container
docker exec <traefik-container> nc -z -w 3 <service-name> <port>
```

If `/proc/net/ip_vs` shows ONLY the header line (no actual rules), the load balancer is broken.

**Common trigger — port conflict thrashing:** A Swarm service with `published:` port conflicts with a standalone container (`docker run`) holding the same host port. The service repeatedly fails to start, corrupting NetworkDB state:
```
Bind for 0.0.0.0:3010 failed: port is already allocated
Peer entry was not in db ... vtep="invalid IP"
endpoint join on GW Network failed
```
This cascades into IPVS rule corruption.

**Fix:**
1. Identify and remove the conflicting standalone container:
   ```bash
   docker ps --format '{{.Names}} {{.Ports}}' | grep <port>
   docker rm -f <rogue-container>
   ```
2. Remove the broken Swarm service:
   ```bash
   docker service rm <conflicting-service>
   ```
3. Restart the Docker daemon to rebuild IPVS tables and NetworkDB:
   ```bash
   systemctl restart docker
   # Wait 10-15s for Swarm to rejoin and services to reschedule
   ```
4. Verify IPVS repopulated:
   ```bash
   sleep 10 && cat /proc/net/ip_vs
   ```

**Prevention:** Never run `docker run -d -p <port>:<port>` on a Swarm-managed node. All services must go through `docker stack deploy` or `docker service create`. If you need a temporary container, use ephemeral ports (`-p 0:<port>`) or run it on a non-Swarm node.

---

## `.env` variables not exported → Docker build args receive empty strings

**Symptom:** `docker build` succeeds but the Next.js app fails at runtime or during prerender with missing environment variables. Build logs show `--build-arg NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=` (empty value) even though `.env` contains the value.

**Root cause:** Shell variables are not automatically exported to subprocesses. When a deploy script does `source ./.env` followed by `docker build --build-arg NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL`, the variable is empty because `source` without `set -a` only sets shell variables, not environment variables.

**Fix:** Wrap the `source` command with `set -a` (export all variables) and `set +a`:
```bash
set -a
source ./.env
set +a
docker build \
  --build-arg NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL="$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL" \
  --build-arg NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY="$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY" \
  -t "$IMAGE_TAG" .
```

**Alternative:** Export each variable explicitly:
```bash
export NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=$(grep '^NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=' .env | cut -d= -f2-)
```

**Prevention:** Always verify build args are non-empty before building:
```bash
if [ -z "$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL" ]; then echo "ERROR: env var not exported"; exit 1; fi
```

**When it breaks:** This manifests most painfully with Next.js prerender. If `/blog` or any SSG page triggers Supabase client initialization during build, and the build arg is empty, the build fails with `supabaseUrl is required` or similar.

---

## Prebuild scripts reference archived/deleted files → build fails with `MODULE_NOT_FOUND`

**Symptom:** `docker build` fails during the prebuild step with `ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND` for a script that lives in `scripts/*.ts` or `scripts/*.mjs`. The script existed in an earlier commit but was archived (moved to `_archive/` or deleted) during a cleanup.

**Root cause:** `package.json` has a `prebuild` script that runs `tsx scripts/generate-X.ts` (or similar), but the script was deleted from HEAD — either intentionally archived, or lost in a merge conflict. The `prebuild` step references the file unconditionally.

**Diagnosis:**
```bash
# Check if the prebuild references a missing file
cat package.json | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('scripts',{}).get('prebuild','NONE'))"

# Check if any generate-X scripts are missing
for script in $(cat package.json | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('scripts',{}).get('prebuild',''))" | grep -oP 'scripts/\w+'); do
  test -f "$script" && echo "EXISTS: $script" || echo "MISSING: $script"
done
```

**Fix — one of:**
1. **Restore from git history** (fastest when the file was accidentally deleted):
   ```bash
   # Find which commit archived the file
   git log --all --diff-filter=D -- scripts/generate-X.ts
   # Restore from the commit before deletion
   git checkout <deletion-commit>^ -- scripts/generate-X.ts
   ```
2. **Skip the prebuild step** (when the script's output file is already committed and nothing has changed):
   ```bash
   # Check if the generated output exists
   test -f lib/engine/generated/renderer-map.ts && echo "output exists - safe to skip"
   # Quick fix: comment out the prebuild in package.json and build directly
   python3 -c "import json; d=json.load(open('package.json')); del d['scripts']['prebuild']; json.dump(d, open('package.json','w'), indent=2)"
   ```
3. **Generate the output manually** (when the script is truly dead):
   ```bash
   # Create a minimal stub that produces the expected output file
   ```

**Prevention:** When archiving scripts, also update `package.json` prebuild to remove references. Always run a full build from scratch (`rm -rf .next && npm run build`) after archiving to catch missing files before they hit production.

**Related — custom base image doesn't exist:**
```dockerfile
FROM paragu-ai/next-base:node20  # this image doesn't exist locally or on any registry
```
Switch to `FROM node:20-alpine` directly when no custom base image is needed. If a custom base is required, build it first with its own Dockerfile (often in a `docker/` subdirectory).

**Recipe — single-stage Dockerfile for a standard Next.js app (no custom base):**
```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app/web
COPY web/package.json web/package-lock.json* ./
RUN npm ci --legacy-peer-deps

FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app/web
COPY --from=deps /app/web/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY web/ .
COPY src/ /app/src/
COPY sites/ /app/sites/
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL
ARG NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL
ENV NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 PORT=3000 HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/web/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/web/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/web/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/src ./src
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/sites ./sites
COPY web/docker-start.sh /app/docker-start.sh
RUN chmod +x /app/docker-start.sh
USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["/app/docker-start.sh"]
```
**Pitfall — `COPY ../` in Dockerfile:** Docker does NOT allow paths above the build context. If `src/` and `sites/` are outside the `web/` directory, build from the repo root and copy with correct relative paths (`COPY web/ .`, then `COPY src/ /app/src/`, `COPY sites/ /app/sites/`).

---

## `reactCompiler: true` without `babel-plugin-react-compiler` installed

**Symptom:** `npx next build` fails with `Module not found: Can't resolve` for every single `@/components/*` import — even though the files exist.

**Root cause:** `next.config.mjs` has `reactCompiler: true` enabled, which requires `babel-plugin-react-compiler` (or equivalent) in `devDependencies`. Without it, Next.js 16's Turbopack fails to resolve ANY path alias, triggering a cascade of bogus `Module not found` errors that look nothing like a missing compiler.

**The real error is easy to miss:** Scroll up past the first few `Module not found` lines — you'll see an earlier error about `Failed to load React compiler` or an unhandled Rust panic (`thread 'tokio-runtime-worker' panicked at ... did not match any character boundary`).

**Fix:**
1. Install the compiler: `npm install --save-dev babel-plugin-react-compiler` (React 19+ only)
2. OR remove `reactCompiler: true` from `next.config.mjs`:

```javascript
// next.config.mjs
// Remove this line:
// reactCompiler: true,
```

**Prevention:** Never add `reactCompiler: true` without installing `babel-plugin-react-compiler` in the same commit. If you copy a `next.config.mjs` from a project that uses React Compiler, remove that config key unless you're also on React 19 and installing the plugin.

---

## Turbopack monorepo lockfile conflict

**Symptom:** `npx next build` aborts early with `Turbopack detected multiple lockfiles` or fails to resolve `@/` aliases with `Module not found: Can't resolve '@/components/...'`.

**Root cause:** Next.js 16 uses Turbopack by default, which scans parent directories for lockfiles. When a repo has a root `package-lock.json` (even if it's stale/ancient) and a `web/package-lock.json`, Turbopack refuses to guess which one to use.

**Fix — add `turbopack.root` to `next.config.mjs`:**

```javascript
// next.config.mjs
const nextConfig = {
  // ...
  turbopack: {
    root: '/absolute/path/to/web',
  },
  // ...
};
```

This tells Turbopack to treat `web/` as the project root and ignore lockfiles in parent directories.

---

## Missing tsconfig.json → all `@/` imports fail

**Symptom:** Next.js 16 build fails with `Module not found: Can't resolve '@/components/...'` for every single component import, even though files exist and the `@` alias is conventional.

**Root cause:** Next.js auto-generates `tsconfig.json` during `next dev`, but in CI/Docker builds it doesn't. Without an explicit `tsconfig.json`, Turbopack has no way to know that `@/*` maps to `./*`. It defaults to treating `@/` as unresolvable.

**Fix — create `web/tsconfig.json`:**

```json
{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2017",
    "lib": ["dom", "dom.iterable", "esnext"],
    "allowJs": true,
    "skipLibCheck": true,
    "strict": true,
    "noEmit": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "module": "esnext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "resolveJsonModule": true,
    "isolatedModules": true,
    "jsx": "preserve",
    "incremental": true,
    "plugins": [{ "name": "next" }],
    "paths": {
      "@/*": ["./*"]
    }
  },
  "include": ["next-env.d.ts", "**/*.ts", "**/*.tsx", ".next/types/**/*.ts"],
  "exclude": ["node_modules"]
}
```

**Verification:**
```bash
# With tsconfig.json present, this should resolve 0 missing modules
npx next build 2>&1 | grep -c "Module not found"
```

---

## `node_modules` too large (1.3GB+) → use pre-built standalone directly

**Situation:** `npm ci` in Docker takes 5+ minutes due to 1.3GB+ `node_modules`. Or the build has complex platform dependencies (sharp, SWC binaries) that fail inside Alpine.

**Strategy — build outside Docker, package `.next/standalone`:**

When `npx next build` (with `output: 'standalone'` in next.config.mjs) already works outside Docker, you can skip the multi-stage Docker build entirely:

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1 PORT=3000 HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs .next/standalone ./
COPY --chown=nextjs:nodejs public ./public
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=40s --retries=3 \
  CMD wget --no-verbose --tries=1 --spider http://localhost:3000/ || exit 1
USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["./docker-start.sh"]
```

Then build and deploy:
```bash
# Build locally (outside Docker, using host node_modules)
cd web && npx next build

# Build Docker image from the standalone output
docker build -f Dockerfile.prod -t paragu-ai:prod .

# Update Swarm service
docker service update --image paragu-ai:prod <stack>_web
```

**Requirements for this to work:**
- `next.config.mjs` must set `output: 'standalone'`
- A `docker-start.sh` entrypoint must exist in the project root (the standalone output copies it)
- `public/` must be copied alongside `.next/standalone`
- The Dockerfile's `.dockerignore` must NOT exclude `.next/`

**When NOT to do this:**
- If you're pushing to a multi-node swarm (other nodes need to pull the image from a registry)
- If the build environment and Docker image differ in platform/arch (build on amd64, run on arm64)
- If you want full reproducibility (no dependency on host node_modules state)

---

## Service-level healthcheck override by old stack — `wget` still running after node:20-slim migration

**Symptom:** You updated the Dockerfile from `node:20-alpine` to `node:20-slim`, added a correct Node HTTP healthcheck in `docker-compose.yml`, rebuilt the image, ran `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml`, and the service reports `starting` indefinitely. The healthcheck keeps failing. Inside the container, the app serves 200 responses fine.

**Root cause:** The old service (deployed from a previous stack version) was created with `wget` in the healthcheck command. `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml` does NOT update the healthcheck configuration of an existing service in all cases — it compares the service spec, and when the healthcheck format changes, Swarm may not detect the diff. The old `wget` healthcheck command remains in the service definition. The new `node:20-slim` image doesn't have `wget`, so every healthcheck probe fails: `exec: "wget": executable file not found in $PATH`.

**Diagnosis:** The container's own inspect shows no healthcheck or a mismatched command:
```bash
docker inspect <CID> --format "{{json .Config.Healthcheck}}"
docker image inspect <image>:<tag> --format "{{json .Config.Healthcheck}}"
docker service inspect <service>_web --format "{{.Spec.TaskTemplate.ContainerSpec.Healthcheck}}"
```

**Fix — update the service healthcheck directly:**
```bash
docker service update \
  --health-cmd "node -e \"require('http').get('http://127.0.0.1:3000/',r=>process.exit(r.statusCode===200?0:1)).on('error',()=>process.exit(1))\"" \
  --health-interval 30s --health-timeout 10s --health-retries 3 --health-start-period 15s \
  <service>_web
```

**Prevention:** When switching from `node:20-alpine` to `node:20-slim`, ALWAYS verify the service healthcheck command after deploy. If it still shows wget, apply the direct update command above.

---

## Quick Swarm service swap (hotfix workflow)

When a stack is already deployed and you only need to swap the image:

```bash
# 1. Build or rebuild the image
docker build -t <service>:prod .

# 2. Update the service directly — no compose file re-parse needed
docker service update --image <service>:prod <stack>_web
```

`docker service update` is faster than `docker stack deploy` because it skips YAML re-parsing and sends only the image digest change to Swarm. Use this for hotfixes; use `docker stack deploy` for structural changes (replicas, ports, env vars, network config).

## Image pushed to registry but service still runs old version

**Symptom:** `docker service update --image new:image:tag` reports success but the running container still serves old code.

**Check:** Did you push to the right registry and tag? Swarm nodes pull from the registry on update. If the image tag exists locally but not on the registry, other nodes will fail to schedule.

**Fix:** Always use registry auth when pushing:
```bash
docker build -t registry.paragu-ai.com/<name>:prod .
docker push registry.paragu-ai.com/<name>:prod
# then update service
docker service update --image registry.paragu-ai.com/<name>:prod <service>
```
For stacks: `docker stack deploy --with-registry-auth -c compose.yml <stack>`.

---

## `docker stack deploy` says "no changes detected" — same image tag, different build

**Symptom:** You ran `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml <stack>` after rebuilding the image with the same tag (`elviajero:prod`). The deploy reports "Skipping: Service <service> has no changes detected" and the container is NOT restarted. The old code still runs.

**Root cause:** Docker Swarm compares the service spec (replicas, image, ports, env, etc.) against the compose file. If the image tag hasn't changed in the compose file, Swarm sees no spec difference and skips the update. It does NOT compare image digests during a stack deploy.

**Fix — force a restart:**

```bash
docker service update --force <stack>_<service>
```

This restarts all tasks regardless of image digest equality, forcing a fresh pull/use of the locally-tagged image.

**Alternative — use unique tags to ensure rollout:**

```bash
GIT_SHA=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)
docker build -t elviajero:prod .
docker tag elviajero:prod elviajero:prod-$GIT_SHA
# Update compose file with new tag, then stack deploy
# Or skip compose and update directly:
docker service update --image elviajero:prod-$GIT_SHA <stack>_web
```

**Single-node Swarm special case:** The warning `image elviajero:prod could be obtained from a registry but image may not be available after node loss` is harmless on single-node Swarms — the image is already cached locally and no other node needs to pull it. Ignore this warning.

---

## `docker service update --image` doesn't apply new code (tag digest unchanged)

**Symptom:** You rebuilt the Docker image (`docker build -t elviajero:prod`), ran `docker service update --image elviajero:prod elviajero_web`, the command reported success, but the container still serves old code. API routes return old behavior, new endpoints 404, or bug fixes don't appear.

**Root cause:** Docker Swarm compares image **digests**, not just tags. If the newly built image overwrites a tag locally but the running task's image digest hasn't changed (or Swarm's internal cache still points to the old digest), the service update becomes a no-op. The container is NOT restarted with the new image.

**Diagnosis:**
```bash
# 1. Check when the running container actually started
docker service ps elviajero_web --format "table {{.Name}}\t{{.CurrentState}}\t{{.Image}}"
# If "Running 25 minutes ago" but you just deployed, it's the old container.

# 2. Compare the image the service thinks it's using vs what's locally available
docker inspect $(docker ps -q -f name=elviajero_web) --format "{{.Image}}"
docker images --format "{{.Repository}}:{{.Tag}} {{.ID}}" | grep elviajero
# If the container's Image ID doesn't match the latest local `elviajero:prod`, it's stale.

# 3. Check the compiled route inside the container
docker exec $(docker ps -q -f name=elviajero_web) \
  grep -n "debug" .next/server/app/api/admin/products/route.js | head -5
# If grep finds nothing, the new code isn't in the container.
```

**Fix -- Force a real update by retagging with a unique tag:**
```bash
# Build with a timestamped tag
docker build -t elviajero:prod-$(date +%s) .
# Or simply retag the latest build to a new name
docker tag $(docker images -q elviajero:prod | head -1) elviajero:prod-latest
# Then update the service with the NEW tag
docker service update --image elviajero:prod-latest elviajero_web
```

**Alternative -- Force update (restarts container even if image appears unchanged):**
```bash
docker service update --force elviajero_web
```
This restarts the task regardless of image digest, but if the local tag still points to the old digest, you get the old code again.

**Best practice for hotfix deploys:**
```bash
# 1. Build
DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build \
  --build-arg NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL="$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL" \
  --build-arg NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY="$NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY" \
  -t elviajero:prod .

# 2. Tag with a unique identifier (timestamp or git sha)
GIT_SHA=$(git rev-parse --short HEAD)
docker tag elviajero:prod elviajero:prod-$GIT_SHA

# 3. Update service with the unique tag
docker service update --image elviajero:prod-$GIT_SHA elviajero_web

# 4. Verify the container restarted with new code
docker service ps elviajero_web
docker exec $(docker ps -q -f name=elviajero_web) grep -c "newFunctionName" .next/server/app/api/some/route.js
```

**Prevention:** Never rely on mutable tags (`:prod`, `:latest`) for production deploys in Swarm. Always use immutable tags (`:prod-[REDACTED_PHONE]` or `:prod-$GIT_SHA`). If you must use `:prod`, verify the container actually restarted by checking `docker service ps` timestamps and by smoke-testing a known-new endpoint or string in the compiled output.

**Related pitfall -- Swarm stack deploy ignores build:**
If using `docker-compose.yml` with a `build:` key, Swarm silently ignores it. You must pre-build, push to a registry, and reference the image by immutable tag in the compose file. See "Swarm stack deploy ignores build key" above.", "file_type": ".md"}

Now I have a clear picture. The session produced several non-trivial deployment lessons that belong under `nextjs-docker-swarm`. Let me update the deployment pitfalls reference with the new learnings.

Key learnings from this session:
1. **Swarm `--force` needed with `docker service update --image`** — even with `--no-cache` build, Swarm doesn't detect tag-only changes. `--force` is required.
2. **`git add -A` traps** — picks up unstaged modified files (video-ads/) while missing source changes. Explicit file listing is safer.
3. **Vercel analytics package causes 404 spam on self-hosted** — `@vercel/analytics` and `@vercel/speed-insights` only work on Vercel platform, not Docker Swarm
4. **LSP stale diagnostics** — after removing imports, LSP still reports errors for the deleted symbols. This is harmless but confusing.
5. **SSR bfcache** — browser restores from back/forward cache showing stale page


**Prevention:** Never rely on mutable tags (`:prod`, `:latest`) for production deploys in Swarm. Always use immutable tags (`:prod-[REDACTED_PHONE]` or `:prod-$GIT_SHA`). If you must use `:prod`, verify the container actually restarted by checking `docker service ps` timestamps and by smoke-testing a known-new endpoint or string in the compiled output.

**Related pitfall — Swarm stack deploy ignores build:**
If using `docker-compose.yml` with a `build:` key, Swarm silently ignores it. You must pre-build, push to a registry, and reference the image by immutable tag in the compose file. See "Swarm stack deploy ignores build key" above.

---

## git add -A picks up unstaged modified files, misses source changes

**Symptom:** You make changes to components and pages, run `git add -A && git commit -m 'fix: overhaul'`, push, deploy. The live site shows NONE of your changes. `git show --name-only HEAD` reveals the commit only contains unrelated files (docs, generated content) — your source files were never included.

**Root cause:** `git add -A` stages ALL changes in the working tree — including unstaged modifications in files you didn't intend to commit (e.g., `video-ads/compositions/*.html` that were modified on the VPS). The `-A` flag does NOT just add new changes; it adds every modified file across the entire tree. If there are untracked or unstaged files in other directories, they can push your intended changes out of the staged set via commit size limits or screen-based add behavior.

**Fix — always add files explicitly for client-site deploys:**
```bash
git add components/header.tsx app/servicios/page.tsx app/page.tsx app/layout.tsx
```

**Verify what's staged before committing:**
```bash
git status --short | grep '^M '   # M in first column = staged
```

Only files with `M` in the FIRST column (staged) will be committed. Files with ` M` (second column only) are unstaged.

**Best practice workflow for client-site changes:**
```bash
# 1. Make your changes
# 2. Verify in browser
# 3. Stage explicitly — one file at a time or a targeted list
git add components/header.tsx app/servicios/page.tsx
# 4. Double-check what's staged
git diff --cached --stat
# 5. Commit
git commit -m 'fix: specific description'
# 6. Push
git push
```

**Prevention:** Never use bare `git add -A` or `git add .` for production deployments. Always list files explicitly. Always run `git status` before committing to verify only intended files are staged.

---

## Vercel analytics/sped-insights packages cause 404 spam on self-hosted

**Symptom:** Browser console floods with 404 errors for `/_vercel/insights/script.js` and `/_vercel/speed-insights/script.js`. Errors are harmless (no functional impact) but create visual noise and could mask real errors. The site works fine.

**Root cause:** `@vercel/analytics` and `@vercel/speed-insights` are Vercel-platform packages. They inject `<script>` tags that load from `/_vercel/*` paths. These paths are served by Vercel's edge infrastructure. On a self-hosted Docker swarm (or any non-Vercel deployment), those paths return 404 from the web server (Traefik/Nginx serves a 404 page in HTML, which fails MIME-type checking for JavaScript).

**Fix — remove the packages entirely:**
```bash
# 1. Remove from package.json
npm uninstall @vercel/analytics @vercel/speed-insights
# 2. Remove imports and JSX from layout.tsx:
#    import { Analytics } from "@vercel/analytics/react"
#    import { SpeedInsights } from "@vercel/speed-insights/next"
#    <Analytics />
#    <SpeedInsights />
```

**Detection:**
```bash
grep -rn 'vercel' app/ components/ --include='*.tsx' --include='*.ts' 2>/dev/null
```

**Prevention:** Do not include Vercel-specific packages in Next.js projects deployed to Docker Swarm. These packages add zero value on non-Vercel infrastructure and only generate console noise.

```bash
# 2. Tag with a unique identifier (timestamp or git sha)

# 3. Update service with the unique tag

```

If you must use `:prod`, verify the container actually restarted by checking `docker service ps` timestamps and by smoke-testing a known-new endpoint or string in the compiled output.

If using `docker-compose.yml` with a `build:` key, Swarm silently ignores it. You must pre-build, push to a registry, and reference the image by immutable tag in the compose file. See "Swarm stack deploy ignores build key" above.

**Symptom:** You make changes to components and pages, run `git add -A && git commit -m 'fix: overhaul'`, push, deploy. The live site shows NONE of your changes. `git show --name-only HEAD` reveals the commit only contains unrelated files (docs, generated content) — your source files were never included.

**Root cause:** `git add -A` stages ALL changes in the working tree — including unstaged modifications in files you didn't intend to commit (e.g., modified composition files in other directories). The `-A` flag does NOT just add new changes; it adds every modified file across the entire tree. If there are unstaged files in other directories, the intended source changes can get displaced or simply not staged if they weren't dirty when the command ran.

**Fix — always add files explicitly for client-site deploys:**
```bash
git add components/header.tsx app/servicios/page.tsx app/page.tsx app/layout.tsx
```

**Verify what's staged before committing:**
```bash
git status --short | grep '^M '   # M in first column = staged
```

Only files with `M` in the FIRST column (staged) will be committed. Files with ` M` (second column only) are unstaged.

**Best practice workflow for client-site changes:**
```bash
# 1. Make your changes
# 2. Verify in browser
# 3. Stage explicitly — one file at a time or a targeted list
git add components/header.tsx app/servicios/page.tsx
# 4. Double-check what's staged
git diff --cached --stat
# 5. Commit
git commit -m 'fix: specific description'
# 6. Push
git push
```

**Prevention:** Never use bare `git add -A` or `git add .` for production deployments. Always list files explicitly. Always run `git status` before committing to verify only intended files are staged.

---

## Vercel analytics/speed-insights packages cause 404 spam on self-hosted Docker

**Symptom:** Browser console floods with 404 errors for `/_vercel/insights/script.js` and `/_vercel/speed-insights/script.js`. The site works fine but console noise hides real errors.

**Root cause:** `@vercel/analytics` and `@vercel/speed-insights` are Vercel-only packages. They inject `<script>` tags loading from `/_vercel/*` paths served by Vercel's edge. On Docker Swarm (or any non-Vercel deployment), these paths return 404 HTML pages, which fail MIME-type checking for JavaScript.

**Fix — remove the packages entirely:**
```bash
# 1. Uninstall
npm uninstall @vercel/analytics @vercel/speed-insights
# 2. Remove from layout.tsx:
#    import { Analytics } from "@vercel/analytics/react"
#    import { SpeedInsights } from "@vercel/speed-insights/next"
#    <Analytics /> and <SpeedInsights /> JSX elements
```

**Prevention:** Do not include Vercel-specific packages in Next.js projects deployed to Docker Swarm. They add no value off-Vercel and only generate console noise.

---

## NEXT_RESH_COMPILE warning — prerender fails when page uses useSearchParams

**Symptom:** Build log shows error about useSearchParams outside Suspense boundary. Caused by pages like `/reservar` that read URL params without boundary. If the page is being redirected anyway (e.g., to WhatsApp), use next.config redirects instead of a page-level implementation.

**Fix:** Delete the page file and add a next.config redirect rule:
```javascript
async redirects() {
  return [
    { source: '/reservar', destination: 'https://wa.me/NUMBER', permanent: true },
  ]
}
```

This avoids the prerender error entirely.

{"action": "write_file", "file_content": "# Session Notes — DepiFlash Mobile Overhaul (May 21, 2026)

## git add -A trap
`git add -A` picks up ALL modified files in the working tree including video-ads composition files that were modified on the VPS. The intended source changes (header, pages, layout) were never staged. The commit only contained video-ads and docs files.

**Fix:** Always use explicit `git add components/header.tsx app/page.tsx ...` for production deploys. Verify with `git status --short | grep '^M '` before committing.

## Swarm force-update required after tag rebuild
`docker build --no-cache -t depiflash:prod .` → `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml depiflash` → OLD container still runs.

Docker Swarm compares service specs against compose file. Same image tag = no spec change = no restart, even though the local image has a new digest. Must use `docker service update --image depiflash:prod depiflash_web --force`.

## Vercel analytics on self-hosted = 404 spam
`@vercel/analytics` and `@vercel/speed-insights` inject `/_vercel/insights/script.js` and `/_vercel/speed-insights/script.js` paths that only exist on Vercel's platform. On Docker Swarm, these return 404 HTML pages that fail JavaScript MIME type checks, flooding console with errors.

**Fix:** Remove packages from package.json, remove imports and `<Analytics />` / `<SpeedInsights />` JSX from layout.tsx.

## /reservar and /admin redirects
Instead of creating page files for redirect routes, use `next.config.ts` `async redirects()`:
```javascript
async redirects() {
  return [{ source: '/reservar', destination: 'https://wa.me/NUMBER?text=...', permanent: true }]
}
```
This avoids build issues with useSearchParams outside Suspense.

## pb-20 on body gets lost during layout.tsx edits
The `pb-20 md:pb-0` on `<body>` was inadvertently removed when patching layout.tsx to remove Vercel analytics. Always verify body className after any edit to layout.tsx.

## bfcache (back/forward cache)
Chrome may restore the page from bfcache after navigation to `/reservar` or `/admin`. Hard refresh (Ctrl+Shift+R) clears it.

**📎 `references/deployment-pitfalls.md`** — deployment edge cases (port conflicts, API route 404s, swarm vs standalone, Traefik integration, Cloudflare DNS token permissions, image hotfix without rebuild). Check this first when you hit an unexplained deploy failure.

## Migration: Tarballs/Workspace to Private npm Registry

**Situation:** The project depends on `@scope/*` packages via `file:.packages/*.tgz` (tarballs) or local workspace packages. You want to switch to the published GitHub Packages registry for cleaner Docker builds and version tracking.

### ⚠️ CRITICAL: Published npm packages may have DIFFERENT APIs than local .tgz

- Published (0.3.1): exports `Hero`, `Header`, `Footer`, `ServicesSection`, `WhatsAppFloat`
- Local `.tgz` (0.1.0): exports `createSectionsRenderer`, 30+ section components, fact
...

Adds analytics (Vercel + GA4), JSON-LD schema (LocalBusiness/ProfessionalService), OG metadata, WhatsApp integration, missing docs (.env.example, README), and verifies all improvements compile, commit, and push. Designed for the Ai-Whisperers fleet of 8+ Next.js client sites. tags: - batch - enhancement - standardization - seo - analytics - ops - client related_skills: - seo-super-agent - professional-services-website - multi-tenant-saas-architecture - cross-repo-architecture-comparison - nexa-content-generation triggers: - improve all sites - batch improvements - standardize client sites - bring sites up to standard - add analytics to every site - add JSON-LD to all sites - site review pass - audit all sites - what can we improve - better all websites - mejorar todos los sitios - analyze what X has that Y does not - what does SITE have that SITE doesn't - compare sites - gap analysis --- # Client Site Enhancement Batch Run a standardized enhancement pass across ALL client sites in a single session. The goal: bring every site to a baseline level of analytics, SEO, schema, and documentation — then commit and push everything. ## Comprehensive Single-Site Audit Pattern When asked to "analyze [site] repo" for a single site (not a batch), produce a structured analysis report with these sections: 1. **Architecture** — next.js version, React, Tailwind, TypeScript, build config (standalone/bundled), Docker setup, Traefik config, number of replicas 2. **Pages** — list every route with page.tsx, note type (static/dynamic/API), mark completeness 3. **Components** — count, list key ones (header, footer, WhatsApp float, etc.) 4. **Features** — analytics, JSON-LD, OG tags, canonical URLs, sitemap, booking systems, admin panels, rate limiting 5. **Issues by severity:** | Level | Color | Meaning | |-------|-------|---------| | 🔴 CRITICAL | Must fix now | Data loss, SEO breakage, legal risk | | 🟡 IMPORTANT | Should fix soon | Performance, UX, maintainability | | 🔵 MENOR | Nice to have | Polish, convenience | Typical critical issues to scan for: - **Sitemap/robots.txt with placeholders** — `SITE_URL_PLACEHOLDER` → site not indexable - **File-based storage without Docker volume** — booking.json, .data/ → data lost on redeploy - **Name/pricing inconsistencies** — testimonio name doesn't match "about" name, outdated prices - **No per-page SEO metadata** — all pages share the same layout title/description 6. **Executive summary table** — score each area (visual/UX/booking/SEO/infra/code) out of 10 ## Cross-Site Analysis Methodology - After onboarding 3+ new builder sites (magnolia, depiflash, luis tier) - Quarterly maintenance pass - When a client mentions they aren't getting traffic (analytics gap is #1 cause) - Before showing a client the dashboard for the first time ## Cross-Site Analysis Methodology When a user says "analyze all the things [Site A] and [Site B] have that [Site C] should implement": - DO NOT delegate to subagents (needs direct file system access) - DO compare: file trees, app/ routes, lib/, components/, API routes - DO produce priority-ranked comparison table - Always check: error/loading/not-found pages, security headers, rate limiting, per-page SEO, content management, admin auth, Docker + healthcheck - Trap: don't propose blog/community features for brochure-style PYME sites. Filter what applies to the site type. **Execution order for a single-site upgrade:** 1. File tree comparison → identify gaps 2. Implement P0 (error pages, security headers, rate limiting if API routes exist) 3. Add per-page SEO metadata (PageMeta component pattern) 4. Add content override system (Supabase + es.json) if the site needs admin editing 5. Hardcoded value extraction (phone, WhatsApp, pricing → JSON → Supabase) 6. Docker + CI verification 7. Commit + push all at once with a comprehensive message ## Enhancement Checklist (Ranked by Impact) ### P0 — Analytics (Vercel + Speed Insights) Every site must have Vercel Analytics and Speed Insights. Without this, you can't show clients traffic data or diagnose issues. **Install:** ```bash npm install @vercel/analytics @vercel/speed-insights ``` For builder sites that use `@ai-whisperers/*` private packages and can't install from npm (401/ETARGET errors), copy the packages from a working site: ```bash cp -r /root/jota-ink-tattoo/node_modules/@vercel node_modules/ ``` Also update `package.json` to add the deps so they persist. **Layout integration:** Add to `app/layout.tsx` imports: ```tsx import { Analytics } from "@vercel/analytics/react" import { SpeedInsights } from "@vercel/speed-insights/next" ``` Add components near ``: ```tsx ``` ### P0 — JSON-LD Schema Add `application/ld+json` script to the layout ``. Type depends on site: | Site type | Schema `@type` | |-----------|----------------| | Beauty salon, tattoo, construction, physical store | `LocalBusiness` | | Software/consulting/professional services | `ProfessionalService` | | E-commerce | `Store` | **Pattern** (insert into `` after theme-color meta): ```tsx ` with the JSON stringified in __html. - **Content JSON access**: Sites that import content JSON (most do) should use `c.site?.title` etc. from the JSON. Sites without a JSON import (nexa, early sites) need hardcoded values. - **nexa uses `ProfessionalService`** not `LocalBusiness` — it's a consulting firm not a physical shop. Also include `email` field. - **elviajero already has Store schema**: Don't duplicate. Only add missing types. - **OG on nexa was completely missing**: Nexa's layout.tsx had no openGraph export at all. Needed full metadata rewrite including title template, description, OG image placeholder, canonical URL. ## Phase 3: Content Management (Post-Enhancement) After the enhancement pass, sites that need admin editing benefit from the **content override pattern**: ### Content Override Pattern (Supabase + es.json) **When:** Client needs to edit content (pricing, FAQ, phone, Instagram) without git. **Architecture:** - `es.json` stays as the renderable baseline - Supabase `ej_site_config` table stores overrides as a single JSONB row (`key="content_overrides"`) - React context fetches overrides at mount, deep-merges with es.json defaults - Admin endpoint writes to the same Supabase row **Changes required per site:** 1. `lib/content-provider.tsx` — context provider with fetch + merge 2. `app/api/content/route.ts` — public GET for overrides 3. `app/api/admin/content/route.ts` — admin PUT (with auth guard) 4. Update components that use hardcoded values to read from `content.*` instead 5. Seed Supabase row: `{ key: "content_overrides", value: { instagram: "...", phone: "..." } }` **See** `multi-tenant-saas-architecture` skill → `references/content-override-pattern.md` for full implementation details. ### Admin Content Editor (El Viajero Pattern) When building the admin content editing UI, create a client-side tree editor: **Editor structure:** - Single page at `/admin/content/page.tsx` with `"use client"` - Fetches current overrides from `/api/content` at mount (GET response: `{ overrides, updatedAt }`) - 4-5 sections rendered as collapsible panels: Business Info, Hero, Pricing/FAQ, SEO - Each section has editable fields (text inputs for strings, JSON editor regions for arrays like pricing zones) - "Save" button PUTs to `/api/admin/content` with `{ overrides: { key: value, ... } }` body - Auth guard on admin routes (redirect to Supabase auth if not logged in) - Confirmation toast on save success **Admin endpoint pattern:** ```tsx // app/api/admin/content/route.ts import { createClient } from "@supabase/supabase-js" const supabase = createClient(url, process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_KEY) export async function GET() { const { data } = await supabase.from("ej_site_config").select("value").eq("key", "content_overrides").single() return NextResponse.json({ overrides: data?.value || {}, updatedAt: data?.updated_at || null }) } export async function PUT(req: Request) { const body = await req.json() const { error } = await supabase.from("ej_site_config").upsert( { key: "content_overrides", value: body.overrides }, { onConflict: "key" } ) if (error) return NextResponse.json({ error: error.message }, { status: 500 }) return NextResponse.json({ success: true }) } ``` **Public content API:** ```tsx // app/api/content/route.ts export async function GET() { const { data } = await supabase.from("ej_site_config").select("value").eq("key", "content_overrides").single() return NextResponse.json({ overrides: data?.value || {}, updatedAt: data?.updated_at || null }) } ``` **ContentProvider context (lib/content-provider.tsx):** - Fetches `/api/content` at mount - Deep-merges overrides on top of es.json defaults - Returns `{ content, loading, error, refetch }` via React context - Caches fetched result in state (no re-fetch on re-render) - Graceful fallback: if API fails, use es.json only ### Batch Color Scheme + Terminology Replacement When the user asks to change brand colors or site-wide terminology: 1. **Inventory:** `grep -rn '#OLD_HEX' app/ components/` to find all files with the old color 2. **Mass replace with one loop:** ```bash for f in app/page.tsx app/servicios/page.tsx components/header.tsx ...; do sed -i 's/#OLD_HEX/#NEW_HEX/g' "$f" done ``` Do ALL colors in the palette in a single loop. 3. **Verify zero old colors remain:** `grep -rn 'OLD_HEX' app/ components/` returns empty 4. **Build + verify:** `npx tsc --noEmit --pretty && npm run build` For **terminology corrections** (medical advice, zone names, product names): 1. Update `content/es.json` first (pricing zones, FAQ answers, page copy) 2. Then update hardcoded strings in TSX files (prep banners, step descriptions, service sections) 3. Add missing FAQ items proactively (e.g. "¿Puedo tomar sol después de la sesión?") 4. Verify: `grep -rn 'TERMINO_VIEJO' app/ content/` returns zero matches **Multi-machine git trap — `git add -A` captures stray local modifications** When working on source changes from one machine (dev environment) and deploying from another (VPS Docker build), explicit `git add ...` is mandatory — `git add -A` will stage ANY locally-modified files (auto-generated compositions, rendered video assets, .next/ output, build artifacts). The resulting commit won't contain your source changes but will contain unrelated files. The VPS then builds from that commit, showing NO source changes. **Always:** ```bash git add components/header.tsx app/page.tsx app/servicios/page.tsx git commit -m 'fix: explicit description' ``` **Never:** `git add -A` when working across machines. After pushing, verify on the deploy machine: ```bash grep -c 'NEW_SYMBOL' components/header.tsx # should be > 0 ``` **Pitfalls when replacing across the fleet:** - Multiple FAQ arrays in es.json — home page and dedicated FAQ page often have separate arrays. Update BOTH. - Hardcoded TSX strings in page.tsx prep banners are distinct from es.json content — fix both independently. - After sed replace, the LSP is often behind. Build with `tsc --noEmit --pretty` to get real errors. - **`"use client"` SSR puzzle:** Pages with `"use client"` render into the RSC (React Server Components) stream as a loading state. The initial HTML will NOT show your hamburger menu, color changes, or any client-specific content — it shows a shell. This is NORMAL. Check the hydrated DOM via `browser_console` or wait for client JS hydration before claiming the deploy didn't work. - The SSR HTML and the JS bundle hash may reference OLD code from Cloudflare cache. Do a hard refresh (Ctrl+Shift+R) on the site before debugging. - Missing `"use client"` directive on pages using `useContent()` hook — will fail at build time. Always check after refactoring from raw import to ContentProvider. - After removing @ai-whisperers/* packages from package.json, also strip the GitHub Packages registry setup from Dockerfile (ARG NODE_AUTH_TOKEN, .npmrc generation script). ### Pitfalls - After adding ContentProvider, pages using `import raw from "@/content/es.json"` still work — they just don't see overrides. Migrate them incrementally. - The `/api/content` route uses service_role key server-side (not exposed to client). - Supabase down = es.json only. Content reverts to defaults gracefully. After the batch pass, content-driven sites (nexa, jota, pitchy tier) need a deeper second pass. This turns a "standard" site into a **production-grade** one. ### Phase 2 Checklist | # | Improvement | Where | Impact | |---|-------------|-------|--------| | 1 | **Error pages** (`error.tsx`, `not-found.tsx`, `loading.tsx`) | `src/app/` root | Professional error handling instead of raw Next.js defaults | | 2 | **Article schema on blog posts** | `blog/[slug]/page.tsx` | Each blog post eligible for rich SERP result (headline, date, author, image) | | 3 | **FaqPage schema** | Pages with FAQ section | Expandable rich accordion in Google search | | 4 | **BreadcrumbList schema** | Every page | Breadcrumb path in SERP, improves site structure signals | | 5 | **OG image** | `public/images/og-default.svg` | Social share preview (create SVG if no real image) | | 6 | **Share buttons** | Blog post template | WhatsApp, X, LinkedIn, Facebook — critical for Paraguay (WhatsApp) | | 7 | **Blog pagination + category filtering** | Blog section component | Don't dump all posts at once; allow filtering | | 8 | **Loading skeletons** | Suspense fallbacks for async routes | Smooth loading instead of blank flash | | 9 | **GSC verification tag** | Root layout metadata | Ready for Google Search Console setup | | 10 | **Rate limiting** | Contact + subscribe API routes | Prevent form spam (10 req/hour/IP bucket pattern) | ### Pattern: lib/schemas.ts Create a shared schema utility for consistent JSON-LD generation: ```tsx // src/lib/schemas.ts interface FaqItem { q?: string; pregunta?: string; question?: string; title?: string; a?: string; respuesta?: string; answer?: string; description?: string; } export function generateBreadcrumbSchema(baseUrl: string, currentUrl: string, pageName: string) { return { "@context": "https://schema.org", "@type": "BreadcrumbList", itemListElement: [ { "@type": "ListItem", position: 1, name: "Inicio", item: baseUrl }, { "@type": "ListItem", position: 2, name: pageName, item: currentUrl }, ], } } export function generateFaqSchema(items: FaqItem[]) { return { "@context": "https://schema.org", "@type": "FAQPage", mainEntity: items.map(item => ({ "@type": "Question", name: item.q || item.pregunta || item.question || item.title || '', acceptedAnswer: { "@type": "Answer", text: item.a || item.respuesta || item.answer || item.description || '' }, })), } } ``` ### Pattern: Blog Post Page with All Enhancements The blog post template should include: 1. Article JSON-LD schema in `` 2. BreadcrumbList JSON-LD 3. OG metadata with `type: 'article'`, `publishedTime`, `authors` 4. ShareButtons component after content 5. Tags display 6. Back-to-blog link 7. Suspense wrapper with loading skeleton fallback 8. Full `generateMetadata()` with `openGraph.images` See `references/blog-post-page-pattern.md` for the complete template. ### Pattern: ShareButtons Component A `'use client'` component with 4 share targets — critical for WhatsApp dominance in PY. ```tsx export function ShareButtons({ url, title }: { url: string; title: string }) { const encodedUrl = encodeURIComponent(url) const encodedTitle = encodeURIComponent(title) // WhatsApp: wa.me/?text= // X: twitter.com/intent/tweet // LinkedIn: linkedin.com/sharing/share-offsite // Facebook: facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php return (...) // SVG icons + onClick → window.open } ``` ### Pattern: Blog Pagination + Category Filter Convert a static blog section to client-state-driven pagination: - `POSTS_PER_PAGE = 9` - `categories` extracted from post data (unique set) - Filter buttons for each category - `currentPage` + `totalPages` state - Previous/Next controls with page indicator For i18n: use a labels map per locale (labels for "Anterior/Siguiente/Página"). ### Pattern: Rate Limiting on API Routes IP-based in-memory buckets. Same code pattern for any route: ```tsx const RATE_LIMIT_WINDOW_MS = 60 * 60 * 1000 const RATE_LIMIT_MAX = 10 const ipBuckets = new Map() function getRateLimitInfo(ip: string) { // Returns { allowed, remaining, resetAt } // On rate limit: return 429 with Retry-After header } ``` ### OG Image (SVG Fallback) When no real OG image exists, create a minimal branded SVG: ```svg ``` ### Phase 2 Order of Execution 1. Error/not-found/loading pages (fast win) 2. Create `lib/schemas.ts` for shared JSON-LD generation 3. Update generic `[slug]/page.tsx` to inject BreadcrumbList + FaqPage schemas 4. Replace `blog/[slug]/page.tsx` with full Article + ShareButtons + Loading skeleton version 5. Replace `BlogSection` component with pagination + category filter version 6. Add OG image SVG to `public/images/` 7. Add GSC verification tag to root layout metadata 8. Add rate limiting to contact + subscribe API routes 9. Commit all with message: `feat: error pages, JSON-LD schemas, OG image, share buttons, blog pagination, rate limiting, GSC verification` ## Docker Persistence Pitfall **File-based storage in Docker Swarm:** Next.js apps that write state to the local filesystem (e.g. `.data/booking.json`, `.data/appointments.db`) **lose all data on every `docker stack deploy`** unless a Docker volume is mounted at that path. **Diagnosis check:** if the site has API routes that write files (booking systems, CMS, file-based counters), look at `docker-compose.yml` for volumes. No volume = data loss on deploy. **Fix per site:** 1. Ensure the file path uses a Docker volume mount in `docker-compose.yml`: ```yaml volumes: booking-data: services: web: volumes: - booking-data:/app/.data ``` 2. Or migrate to Supabase/PostgreSQL for stateful data and keep the filesystem read-only. **Pattern for file-based booking:** If the client doesn't need persistent storage across different machines (single-replica site), use a Docker volume. If the site runs 2+ replicas, **must use a database** (file volumes shared across replicas have race conditions on concurrent writes). ## Reference: 8-Site Summary Table | Site | Analytics | JSON-LD | OG | WhatsApp | .env.example | README | |------|-----------|---------|-----|----------|-------------|--------| | elviajero-comercio | Had GA4, add Vercel | Already had Store | Had basic | Had | Had | Add | | nexa-paraguay | Had GA4, add Vercel | Add ProfessionalService | Add full OG | Had | Had | Add | | jota-ink-tattoo | Add Vercel | Add LocalBusiness | Already had | Had | Had | Had | | dayah-litworks | Add Vercel | Already had | Already had | Add float | Had | Had | | pitchy-website | Add Vercel | Add LocalBusiness | Already had | Had | Add | Had | | magnolia-peluqueria | Add Vercel | Already had | Already had | Had | Add | Had | | depiflash | Add Vercel | Already had | Already had | Had | Add | Had | | luis-de-leon-concept | Add Vercel | Already had | Already had | Had | Add | Had | ## Commit Pattern ``` feat: add Vercel Analytics, JSON-LD schema, WhatsApp float, .env.example, README ``` Push to all repos after committing. Each site has its own repo at `Ai-Whisperers/`. {"action": "write_file", "file_content": "# Vercel Analytics on Self-Hosted Docker — Do NOT Use

## Problem

`@vercel/analytics` and `@vercel/speed-insights` inject `<script>` tags that load `/_vercel/insights/script.js` and `/_vercel/speed-insights/script.js`. These paths **only exist on Vercel's platform**. On Docker Swarm (or any non-Vercel deployment), they return 404 HTML pages which fail JavaScript MIME-type checks. The browser console floods with:

```
Refused to execute script from 'https://site.com/_vercel/analytics/script.js' because its MIME type ('text/html') is not executable
```

## Condition

Only add Vercel analytics on sites deployed to Vercel. Self-hosted Docker Swarm sites should use Google Analytics 4 (GA4) via gtag directly in the layout head.

## Detection

- `docker-compose.yml` exists at repo root → Docker Swarm
- `vercel.json` exists → Vercel
- Ask the user

## Fix — Remove from self-hosted sites

```bash
npm uninstall @vercel/analytics @vercel/speed-insights
```

Then remove from `app/layout.tsx`:
- Delete `import { Analytics } from "@vercel/analytics/react"`
- Delete `import { SpeedInsights } from "@vercel/speed-insights/next"`
- Delete `<Analytics />` and `<SpeedInsights />` components near `</body>`

## Replacement — GA4 gtag

Add to `app/layout.tsx` `<head>` section:

```tsx
<script
  async src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/gtag/js?id=G-XXXXXXXXXX"
/>
<script
  dangerouslySetInnerHTML={{
    __html: `window.dataLayer=window.dataLayer||[];function gtag(){dataLayer.push(arguments)}gtag('js',new Date());gtag('config','G-XXXXXXXXXX',{cookie_flags:'max-age=7200;secure;samesite=none'});`
  }}
/>
```

## Pitfall — pb-20 on body gets lost during layout edits

When removing Vercel components, the patch may replace the `<body>` className. If the site previously had `pb-20 md:pb-0` (for mobile CTA bar clearance), verify it's still present after the edit.

## Pitfall — Unused packages in package.json

After uninstalling, verify `package.json` no longer lists `@vercel/analytics` or `@vercel/speed-insights` under dependencies. If the imports were already removed but the packages remain in `package.json`, they're unused but harmless — remove them for cleanliness.

## Skill library update summary

### Updated: `nextjs-docker-swarm` (devops)

**Added reference file:** `references/depiflash-mobile-overhaul-[REDACTED_PHONE].md`

Captures 6 session-specific lessons:
1. **`git add -A` trap** — commits unrelated modified files (video-ads/ compositions) while missing source changes. Always use explicit `git add <files>` for deploys.
2. **Swarm force-update required with mutable tags** — `docker build --no-cache -t app:prod` + `docker stack deploy` does NOT restart containers because Swarm sees no spec change. Must use `docker service update --image app:prod stack_web --force`.
3. **Vercel analytics 404 spam on self-hosted** — `@vercel/analytics` and `@vercel/speed-insights` only work on Vercel platform. Remove from Docker Swarm deployments.
4. **`next.config.ts` redirects for dead routes** — `/reservar` and `/admin` should be 308 redirects to WhatsApp, not page files.
5. **pb-20 preservation during layout edits** — easy to lose the `pb-20 md:pb-0` on `<body>` when patching layout.tsx.
6. **bfcache** — browser may restore stale page from back/forward cache after redirect.

Also updated the Swarm force-update section in `references/deployment-pitfalls.md` (the `--force` pitfall was already there but now promoted to its own section at the top of the existing "same image tag" entry).

### Updated: `client-site-enhancement-batch` (operations)

**Added reference file:** `references/vercel-analytics-on-selfhosted-docker.md`

This is the more important fix — the batch enhancement skill **currently recommends adding Vercel Analytics to every site unconditionally**. That advice is wrong for self-hosted Docker deployments. The new reference file documents:
- When NOT to add Vercel analytics (Docker Swarm = GA4 instead)
- How to detect Vercel vs Docker deployment
- How to remove Vercel packages when migrating off Vercel
- GA4 gtag replacement pattern
- Pitfalls: pb-20 preservation, phantom package.json entries

### Skills NOT updated (by rule)

- `hermes-agent` — protected bundled skill
- `professional-services-website` — covers entirely different class of work (site scaffolding, not deployment)

### Nothing else to save.

The session produced no user-preference corrections (Ivan's tone signals were the same as always: direct, short, wants fixes not status). The workflow corrections were deployment-specific (git add, swarm force, vercel packages) and are now captured. No loaded skill was consulted that turned out wrong — the existing deployment-pitfalls.md already had partial coverage of the force-update issue, I just extended it.
